Skill Demand and Supply in Poland 2025

Insights from Online Job Postings and Trainings

Published

April 1, 2026

Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This report analyses labour demand and supply in Poland through the lens of online job advertisements and training provision data. It combines web-scraped vacancy data from Pracuj.pl, the largest online job portal in Poland, with information from the Baza Usług Rozwojowych (BUR), the country’s main database of training and upskilling services. The analysis focuses on how employers signal required skills and how these relate to the skills offered through the training system in 2025.

The study integrates labour market analysis with modern NLP methods, including automated text processing, classification, and LLM-assisted skill extraction, to transform unstructured job postings and training descriptions into comparable measures of skill demand and skill supply.

Using ESCO-based mappings, the report compares the composition of requested and supplied skills across occupations, with particular attention to digital competencies and emerging skill needs. It also introduces indicators of relative labour demand, derived from job posting intensity, and contrasts them with the structure of available training.

The Polish labour market is undergoing rapid transformation, with increasing digitalization shaping both hiring practices and skill development pathways. Online job postings provide a timely proxy for employer demand, while training data from BUR offers insight into the supply of skills and reskilling opportunities, enabling a joint assessment of potential mismatches between demand and supply.

Regional Coverage

Relative labour demand

The map shows regional demand and training density normalized by the average labour force in 2025. Use the switch above the map to change the view between job offers and BUR training services.

Regional comparison table

Counts are shown alongside their labour-force-normalized counterparts to make regional demand and supply comparable across differently sized voivodeships. Click a column header to sort the table.

Quick read

Key regional patterns

Data and Methodology

Data collection

We collect job advertisements from Pracuj.pl, the largest online job portal in Poland, and training-service listings from Baza Usług Rozwojowych, the country’s main public database of training and upskilling services. The analytical sample shown in this report focuses on records that can be assigned to Polish voivodeships and linked to the regional demand and supply comparison.

For job advertisements, we retain records with sufficient textual content to support downstream NLP processing, including job titles and descriptions. For BUR, we use training-service records with usable regional metadata and service descriptions. We remove duplicates and standardize core fields before the records enter the analytical pipeline.

Processing pipeline

Raw job postings and training descriptions contain noisy, unstructured information. We transform them into a standardized analytic dataset in six steps: we first identify the source language, translate non-Polish text into Polish, isolate skill-relevant sentences, map extracted skills to ESCO, standardize job titles to ESCO occupations using both titles and skill context for disambiguation, and then enrich each record with regional information and labour-force denominators for voivodeship-level comparison.

01
Language
Detection
Automatic identification of the source language for each job posting or training description
02
Translation
Non-Polish descriptions are translated into Polish before downstream NLP processing
03
Skill-Sentence
Filtering
Skill-relevant fragments are identified in vacancy and training text before structured extraction
04
ESCO
Mapping
Extracted skills are mapped to the ESCO taxonomy to make demand and supply directly comparable
05
Job Title
Standardisation
Job titles are mapped to ESCO occupations using titles and extracted skills as context for disambiguation
06
Data
Augmentation
Records are enriched with NACE sector information and geolocation used for regional assignment

Our pipeline outperforms the current state of the art by approximately 3 percentage points in RP@5 on the HOUSE and TECH benchmark datasets reported by Decorte et al. (2025). These benchmarks consist of job postings annotated with ESCO gold-standard skill labels by human experts, against which model predictions are evaluated. The reported RP@5 scores are 0.822 and 0.838, respectively.

AI Skills Demand

The demand for AI-related skills is highly dynamic, with new technologies and tools emerging rapidly while others become obsolete. This makes it difficult to track AI skill demand using static taxonomies alone.

Standard classifications such as ESCO are not updated frequently enough to capture highly granular and technology-specific skills, such as individual AI tools, models, copilots, or chatbot platforms. At the same time, ESCO provides a robust and interpretable structure through broader concepts such as machine learning, natural language processing, computer vision, and digital content creation.

To address this trade-off, we adopt a hybrid approach inspired by Martins-Neto et al. (2026), who use Lightcast AI skills to track digital-skill demand. In their application, the AI skill list contains 257 items. Because the AI skill space evolves quickly, we extend this approach with a newer and broader Lightcast AI list containing 361 skills, allowing us to capture more recent references to specific tools, platforms, and frameworks.

Lightcast’s taxonomy is substantially more granular than ESCO and is therefore well suited to detecting early signals of technological change. However, relying only on highly specific Lightcast labels would lead to fragmented and less interpretable analysis, limiting comparability across time, occupations, and regions.

We therefore use a two-step strategy:

  1. Lightcast AI skills are used to identify and track emerging AI signals in job postings.
  2. These signals are mapped to broader ESCO categories to enable consistent aggregation and comparison.

The resulting bridge between Lightcast and ESCO is constructed using semantic similarity methods based on embeddings of both skill labels and descriptions from the two taxonomies. In practice, we embed Lightcast AI skills and a targeted ESCO subset combining the full S5 working with computers branch with ICT- and digital-related ESCO knowledge concepts. We then identify the closest conceptual matches between the detailed Lightcast skill entries and broader ESCO categories. This allows us to combine the granularity of real-time skill signals with the stability and interpretability of a standardized taxonomy, enabling robust analysis of AI skill demand in a rapidly evolving technological landscape.

The figure below summarizes the conservative bridge used in the final AI-demand counts. The 361 Lightcast AI skills are mapped to a restricted set of 22 AI-specific ESCO concepts: 18 knowledge concepts and 4 skills concepts. This more selective bridge avoids broad non-AI categories and is used to identify job postings that contain at least one detailed AI skill signal.

Input taxonomy
361 Lightcast AI skills
Granular tools, models, platforms, frameworks, and AI methods
→
Semantic bridge
Embedding-based matching
Labels + descriptions embedded with voyage-4-large
→
Filtered ESCO layer
22 AI-specific ESCO concepts
Only AI-relevant ESCO concepts are retained as valid bridge targets
→
Offer-level metric
AI-skill flag per posting
A posting is AI-related if it contains at least one skill matched to the final AI bridge
Knowledge concepts 18 concepts · 296 matched Lightcast skills
0619.12 Principles of artificial intelligence 0231.4 Natural language processing 0612.47.0 Data mining methods 0714.62 Robotics T6.6.2.0.3 Data ethics
Skills concepts 4 concepts · 65 matched Lightcast skills
S5.5.2.5.1 Analyse big data S5.1.0.29 Utilise machine learning S5.1.0.33 Build recommender systems S5.1.0.9 Perform dimensionality reduction

A few examples from our Lightcast-to-ESCO bridge illustrate the logic of the mapping:

Lightcast AI skill ESCO match Interpretation
3D Reconstruction image recognition A highly specific computer-vision signal is mapped to a clearly AI-specific ESCO concept rather than to a broader imaging task.
AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) machine learning A named algorithm is aggregated into a broader and interpretable AI method category.
Aforge computer vision Tool- or library-specific terminology is mapped to the wider technical domain it represents.
AI Assisted Content Generation utilise machine learning Generative-AI terminology is retained within the final bridge only when it resolves to an explicitly AI-related ESCO concept.
AI Agents principles of artificial intelligence New agentic-AI vocabulary is grouped under a broader conceptual AI category in ESCO.

Source: Lightcast AI skills taxonomy; ESCO; authors' semantic bridge based on embedding similarity.

Results

Digital Skill Levels in Poland

Using the classification proposed by Angel-Urdinola, Chinen, and Rodon Casarramona (2025), we group ESCO-linked digital skills into basic, intermediate, and advanced categories. Across Poland, 696,092 digital skill mentions from job postings can be classified in this way. The resulting profile shows that advanced digital skills account for the largest share of classified digital demand.

Figure 5. Distribution of classified digital skill mentions in Poland
696,092 classified digital skill mentions
43.45%
33.13%
23.42%
Advanced 43.45%
Basic 33.13%
Intermediate 23.42%

Source: Angel-Urdinola, D., Chinen, M. H., and Rodon Casarramona, G. 2025. Digital Skills Development: Competence Frameworks, Assessment Tools, and Pedagogical Approaches. Education Notes, 203091; Pracuj.pl job postings; authors' mapping of classified ESCO-linked digital skill mentions.

AI Demand Snapshot

Share of all job postings with at least one AI skill

1.08%

8,116 of 749,569 postings under the conservative Lightcast-to-ESCO bridge.

Figure 4. Most demanded AI skills and knowledge in Poland
S Skill K Knowledge
S Analyse big data
62.90%
K Principles of artificial intelligence
25.67%
K Natural language processing
5.84%
S Utilise machine learning
3.60%
K Data mining methods
2.24%
K Robotics
1.32%
K Data ethics
1.15%
K Image recognition
0.92%
K Information extraction
0.80%
K Decision support systems
0.79%

Source: Pracuj.pl job postings; Lightcast AI skills taxonomy; ESCO; authors' semantic bridge based on embedding similarity. Values show the share of AI postings containing each skill or knowledge concept.

Regional AI Demand

The map below shows AI demand intensity by voivodeship, measured as the number of postings containing at least one AI skill under the final Lightcast-to-ESCO bridge, normalized by the 2025 labour force. Click a voivodeship to inspect its three most characteristic AI skills. To identify AI skills and knowledge that are particularly characteristic of individual voivodeships, we apply the log-likelihood ratio (G²) test following Dunning (1993), comparing each region with the rest of the country.

References

Angel-Urdinola, D., Chinen, M. H., and Rodon Casarramona, G. 2025. Digital Skills Development: Competence Frameworks, Assessment Tools, and Pedagogical Approaches. Education Notes, 203091.

Decorte, J. J., Van Hautte, J., Develder, C., and Demeester, T. 2025. Efficient Text Encoders for Labor Market Analysis. IEEE Access.

Dunning, T. 1993. Accurate Methods for the Statistics of Surprise and Coincidence. Computational Linguistics 19(1): 61-74.

Martins-Neto, A., Liu, Y., Khurana, S., and Porras Lopez, J. M. 2026. Click, Code, Earn: The Returns to Digital Skills. World Bank.
